attributes.stem:
A 74-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a fall and is admitted to the general medical ward. He has been in hospital for two days. His family is concerned about his ability to manage at home following his wife's death three months ago. They report he appears unkempt, has reduced appetite, and appears to have increased his alcohol consumption, which he denies. He has a medical history of hypertension, dyslipidaemia, osteoarthritis, and depression. His regular medications are irbesartan, paracetamol slow release, and atorvastatin.

On examination, he appears confused and mildly agitated. His pulse rate is 98/min, blood pressure 133/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 96% on room air, and temperature 37.2B0C. There is no evidence of head injury or chest trauma. He is mobilising independently on the ward but appears disoriented to time, place and person. He appears agitated and is verbally combative.

His chest X-ray at admission showed no rib fractures and clear lung fields and his non-contrast CT brain revealed no acute intracranial haemorrhage. Blood tests taken this morning show:

  • C-reactive protein 7 mg/L (0-5)

  • Point of care CBE: within normal limits.

  • VBG: pH 7.46 (7.35-7.45), pCO2 33 mmHg (35-45), HCO3- 23 mmol/L (22-26), Lactate 2.0 mmol/L (0.5-2.2).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Referral to consult liaison psychiatry service
Initiate oral diazepam as per an alcohol withdrawal scale
attributes.explanation:
This clinical picture is consistent with alcohol withdrawal delirium. Given his recent bereavement, the family's concerns for increased alcohol intake and current presentation of confusion and agitation after two days in hospital, alcohol withdrawal is the most likely diagnosis.

Initiate oral diazepam as per an alcohol withdrawal scale is the most appropriate next step in management. Benzodiazepines are the first-line treatment for alcohol withdrawal. They help prevent serious complications such as seizure or progressive delirium. Diazepam is often preferred due to its long half-life, which provides a smoother withdrawal. The dose can be adjusted based on clinical observation using an alcohol withdrawal scale, which is a structured assessment tool that evaluates and scores withdrawal symptoms to guide treatment decisions and medication dosing. This scale includes symptoms such as tremor, sweating, anxiety, agitation, and hallucinations.

Complete septic screen might be considered given the mildly elevated C-reactive protein. However, the clinical picture is more consistent with alcohol withdrawal than sepsis. The patient's vital signs are relatively stable, point of care bloods are fairly unremarkable and there are no clear signs of infection. While infection should remain on the differential diagnosis, managing the likely alcohol withdrawal takes priority, and any further work up can be performed after he is more stable.

Initiate oral thiamine is incorrect as thiamine should be given intravenously rather than orally in suspected alcohol withdrawal. Intravenous thiamine is essential to prevent Wernicke's encephalopathy, particularly in patients with poor nutritional status. However, managing the acute withdrawal symptoms with benzodiazepines takes priority.

Referral to consult liaison psychiatry service may be beneficial for this patient, particularly given his history of depression and recent bereavement. However, it is not the most urgent next step. The immediate priority is to manage the acute alcohol withdrawal symptoms.

Initiate oral quetiapine is not the most appropriate first step. While quetiapine can be used to manage agitation or hallucinations in some forms of delirium, it is not recommended as a first-line treatment for alcohol withdrawal. It does not prevent the progression of withdrawal symptoms and may increase the risk of complications.

In addition to pharmacological management with benzodiazepines, it's crucial to provide supportive care, including fluid and electrolyte replacement, intravenous thiamine supplementation, and close monitoring of vital signs and withdrawal symptoms. The underlying causes of increased alcohol use, such as bereavement and depression, should also be addressed once the acute withdrawal phase has been managed.
learningPoints: Alcohol withdrawal is a common cause of delirium in older adults with a history of alcohol use disorder.
Benzodiazepines are the first-line treatment for alcohol withdrawal, with diazepam preferred in patients without liver disease.
Thiamine should be given intravenously in suspected alcohol withdrawal to prevent Wernicke's encephalopathy.
references: The ASAM Clinical Practice Guideline on Alcohol Withdrawal Management [published correction appears in J Addict Med. 2020 Sep/Oct;14(5):e280. doi: 10.1097/ADM.0000000000000731]. J Addict Med. 2020;14(3S Suppl 1):1-72. Available from: https://www.asam.org/quality-care/clinical-guidelines/alcohol-withdrawal-management-guideline

Haber PS, Riordan BC, Winter DT, et al. New Australian guidelines for the treatment of alcohol problems: an overview of recommendations. Med J Aust. 2021 Oct 4;215 Suppl 7:S3-S32. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34601742/

attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree

attributes.description: Apply, analyze, evaluate, and create (implementing, organizing, and critiquing information)
